author: @shaunh4714
text: As you've pointed out many times,  cost effective wind generation in urban areas is dependant on the ability to generate in low wind speeds. I plan a DIY attempt in the new year and I'm playing with ideas. One of the options I am considering is combining multiple aeromine "wing pairs" to suck air through one  propeller generator. The air duct at the bottom of each "wing pair" would join to a single tube where propeller would sit.  Does anyone have an opinion on wether that would result in the propellor generating in lower winds than with a single "wing pair" aeormine setup? The idea is to concentrate availalbe  wind to a single generator.

author: @darrellbedford4857
text: An interesting concept. My question is the stationary design. Conventional Windmills can rotate the power head (nacelles) to face the blades into the prevailing wind. How does the Aeromine adapt t the changing wind direction. If the blades are extremely tall the will need to be supported with guy wires, These wires will not let the unit turn to face into the wind for best performance.

author: @denarjan
text: Doesn't the design of aerospike engines show that there is no need for two planes in parallel? It Would cut down the height of the construction tremendously fixing it horizontal, and the single wing would see much less reduced air speed. Its the air speed that causes the lower air pressure in the first place. And should the wing be fixed at all? Or could it be made a floating design suspended between wires connected to these artificial muscles I saw presented on the channel. Or fix the wing on an axle through the thickest part as the hinge point and connect it a little ways down to an oil cylinder with an open end pushing or pulling the oil through a pump/generator. Even that kind of design could still make use of the artificial muscle used in small short bunches as maximum flutter range limiters. They use push pull generators in the harvesting of wave energy, robust and able to handle a wide range of pressures.

author: @alexwild4350
text:
2:50 minutes in - There's nothing wrong with being called "Brilliant but Slow". I had a girlfriend that used to call me that all the time.
The F1 or F16 [I'm a bit slow] aerofoils have them there holes  for creating laminar air flow up to and beyond that point if there is a vacuum on the other end of the tube, though I suspect the holes are way to large for this.
There was a plane made years ago that had tiny holes all over the wing, and the intake of a small jet engine was hooked up to the void in the wing. The effect of this 'suction' was to prolong the laminar air flow over the wing just about doubling the performance, and thereby reducing the fuel used by probably four times. Why didn't it catch on ? The difficulty in keeping the holes clean and clear, as once blocked performance downgraded to normality and there were thousands of tiny holes all over the wings - a maintenance nightmare. 
One cannot take a wing from a full sized aircraft and scale this down to model sizes and expect the same performance. It don't work like that. For small scale wings you need a NACA aerofoil shape designed at the outset for the size that you want, then at the wind speeds you expect to use it on. It is called the Reynolds number cause a Mr Reynolds invented the scale.
There was nothing intimidating about building up model aircraft wings out of Balsa wood on the kitchen table. Ask my mum if you want to know. Did it all the time as a young lad. Builders foam is probably the quickest and cheapest method of construction with a skin of Obeechie wood as the covering. Done in a  couple of hours rather than days and at high cost of the filament printer. Check your local well stocked model store or online.

author: @hibraisil
text: Have you ever watched Burt Rutan's tutorial on making airfoils for the Varieze kitplanes? It's a composite that is far less complicated than the standard construction with ribs and spars. Simple airfoils like these are ideal for that method, which is basically hot wire foam work and fiberglass.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: Thank you for another interesting look at these " bladeless " turbines.  I looked at the shape you printed and with a few modifications, particularly making it round instead of a flat blade, you end up with the Dyson fan working as suction.  I still believe people are missing how to scale up such turbine systems.  they do not need bigger, they need more.  At ground level the wind may be slow, at the first floor level it can be higher and by the height of a second story could be in the opposite direction and a different speed.  That is not a problem for stacks of short vertical wind generators.

author: @eelcohoogendoorn8044
text: I doubt it will beat a regular design in terms of power extracted from a given amount of wind, given the indirectness of the power transfer. However, from a mechanical complexity perspective, it has a lot going for it. Rather than the entire structure being something mobile, supported by bearings, vibrations and what not; the large bit is a nice and boring static bit of geometry; and all the air->electricity conversion can happen in a nice and focussed small volume of high power density. That is rather neat.

One part of the Aeromine was missing in this demonstration, that being the cylinder between and slightly forward of the fins, perhaps that increases intake and pressures. I don't know, and Aeromine is being secretive.

author: @Grynneman1
text:
Great video again.  I think the Aeromine (eg the one being marketed at the moment with the central pipe between two aerofoils)
is primarily being tested/installed on large warehouse type buildings, sat on a roof/wall edge facing the prevailing wind direction.   If they are installed in close proximity to each other along that leading edge, they should catch the upward flow of air hitting that wall.

author: @jerrodlopes186
text: Aeromines are a fun concept but so reliant on factors that are not within the scope of one's control. I'm no expert on aeromines, mind you. Knowing a bit about airfoils, though, there are factors such as air density/humidity that play a significant role in optimal airfoil performance. Designs could certainly be made to adapt to these factors, but it seems like an awful lot of work, to me, when there exists numerous other ways to go about doing it.

replies.text: The dimples on the golf balls are there to create turbulence to achieve a desired effect. For this application they are counterproductive. The smoother the better.
